skill.name: busco-phylogeny
skill.description: Generate phylogenomics workflows from genome assemblies using BUSCO or compleasm single-copy orthologs, with scripts for local, SLURM, and PBS environments.
skill.summary: Build scheduler-aware BUSCO phylogenomics workflows from local or NCBI genome assemblies.
skill.author: brunoasm

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, and network patterns. Most weak-crypto, path-traversal, hidden-file, and recursive-delete alerts are documentation or normal workflow examples, but shell wrappers construct commands and execute them with eval, which is a real injection risk if filenames or option values are untrusted. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential theft, persistence, or confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Shell eval Used for External Tool Wrappers
high_findings.description: The Aliscore and ALICUT wrappers build command strings and execute them with eval. Several command components come from filenames or CLI option values, so crafted alignment paths or option values could alter the shell command when these scripts are run.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky pattern is confirmed in executable shell scripts, not only in documentation. Exploitability depends on whether users run the wrappers on untrusted file names or option values.
high_findings.remediation: Replace eval with direct array-based command execution and validate numeric options, tree paths, and outgroup values before invoking Perl tools.

high_findings.title: Bundled Third-Party Perl Tools Require Review
high_findings.description: The skill bundles predownloaded Aliscore and ALICUT Perl code. Static analysis flagged many weak-algorithm and reconnaissance-looking patterns in those bundled files, and marketplace users may execute them as part of generated workflows.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tools appear to be domain-specific legacy bioinformatics utilities, not confirmed malware. The risk remains high because bundled third-party executable code is large and should be provenance-verified before publication.
high_findings.remediation: Publish only after verifying upstream provenance, checksums, licensing, and a focused code review of the bundled Perl utilities.

medium_findings.title: Network Access to NCBI and External Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally queries NCBI datasets APIs and runs the NCBI datasets CLI to download genome assemblies. This is expected for the workflow, but it creates network dependency and data disclosure considerations for submitted taxon names and accessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit and aligned with the advertised NCBI-download feature. It is not exfiltration, but users should know external services receive query terms and accessions.
medium_findings.remediation: Document outbound network use and require user confirmation before downloading or querying remote services.

medium_findings.title: Workflow Scripts Write, Move, Copy, and Symlink Files
medium_findings.description: Several helper scripts create output directories, copy FASTA files, rename genome files, and create symlinks for untrimmed ALICUT results. This is legitimate workflow behavior, but it can overwrite or move files if users run scripts in the wrong directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are direct and intentional. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the scripts act on user-selected workflow files.
medium_findings.remediation: Keep dry-run modes where possible, warn users to run in a project directory, and avoid following untrusted symlinks.

medium_findings.title: Scheduler Templates Source User Shell Startup Files
medium_findings.description: Local, SLURM, and PBS templates source ~/.bashrc before activating conda. This is common on clusters, but it executes user shell startup code in generated jobs and was correctly flagged as hidden-file access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is visible and conventional for conda initialization, but it can execute arbitrary local shell startup content when jobs run.
medium_findings.remediation: Prefer explicit conda initialization paths or let users choose whether generated job files source shell startup files.

low_findings.title: Many Static Alerts Are Documentation or Domain False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts in README content are false positives from ordinary words such as methods. The recursive delete findings inspected in the reference are cleanup commands for /tmp/phylo_downloads, not deletion of root or home directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual context showed these examples do not implement cryptography or destructive root/home deletion. They should not drive the final risk level.
low_findings.remediation: Tune static rules to distinguish prose and bounded temporary-directory cleanup from executable security findings.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: A repository search for common prompt-injection phrases and authority-override language found no matching content. No file contents attempted to override audit instructions or claim preapproval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The search covered the repository for common injection terms, and the skill header and purpose text are normal domain instructions. Absence of evidence does not replace full manual review of every prose line.
low_findings.remediation: Continue prompt-injection screening during marketplace ingestion.

dangerous_patterns.title: eval ${ALISCORE_CMD}
dangerous_patterns.description: The script executes a constructed command string using eval after appending parsed CLI options.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a direct shell evaluation pattern in an executable script. It is the strongest confirmed risk in the skill.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Use a Bash array such as cmd=(perl -I "$SCRIPT_DIR" "$ALISCORE_SCRIPT" -i "$(basename "$ALIGNMENT")") and execute "${cmd[@]}".

dangerous_patterns.title: eval ${ALICUT_CMD}
dangerous_patterns.description: The script executes a constructed ALICUT command string with eval in silent mode.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The options are limited flags, but eval is still unnecessary and unsafe in a user-facing wrapper.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Invoke Perl directly with quoted arguments or a Bash array.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Downloads Through NCBI CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The downloader invokes the datasets CLI to retrieve genome archives from remote services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call uses an argument list, which lowers injection risk, but it performs network downloads and stores external data locally.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Document the download destination and require trusted accession inputs.

content.value_statement: Phylogenomics workflows are hard to assemble across local files, NCBI accessions, and cluster schedulers. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through reproducible BUSCO or compleasm workflow generation.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through required inputs for BUSCO or compleasm phylogenomics projects.
Generates workflow structure for genome download, ortholog extraction, alignment, trimming, concatenation, and tree inference.
Provides local, SLURM, and PBS templates for selected workflow stages.
Includes helper scripts for NCBI assembly queries, genome downloads, renaming, QC reports, and ortholog extraction.
Documents lineage selection, resource planning, trimming options, and substitution model considerations.
Supports publication-oriented methods summaries for completed workflows.
limitations: It does not run analyses automatically unless users execute the generated scripts.
Generated scripts require installed bioinformatics tools such as compleasm, MAFFT, IQ-TREE, and ASTRAL.
NCBI query and download steps require network access and compatible NCBI datasets packages.
Bundled legacy Aliscore and ALICUT utilities need provenance review before marketplace publication.

use_cases.title: Plan a Genome Phylogeny Project
use_cases.description: Create a stepwise workflow from genome assemblies to concatenated and species-tree analyses.

use_cases.title: Prepare Cluster Job Scripts
use_cases.description: Adapt SLURM or PBS templates for compleasm, partition search, and gene tree batches.

use_cases.title: Teach Phylogenomics Workflow Design
use_cases.description: Explain each major pipeline stage and produce scripts with clear directory organization.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a BUSCO phylogenomics workflow for 12 local genome assemblies on my workstation.

prompt_templates.title: Use NCBI Accessions
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a workflow that queries NCBI for assemblies from my target taxon and downloads selected genomes before analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Scheduler Scripts
prompt_templates.prompt: Create SLURM scripts for a compleasm-based phylogenomics project with 40 genomes, 64 total cores, and array jobs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Full Publication Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a complete phylogenomics pipeline with QC thresholds, trimming choices, partition search, IQ-TREE inference, ASTRAL species tree, and methods text.

output_examples.input: User asks for a local BUSCO phylogeny workflow for 20 genomes.
output_examples.output: A directory plan, required questions, tool checklist, and ordered commands for genome QC, ortholog extraction, alignment, trimming, and tree inference.

output_examples.input: User asks to run the workflow on a SLURM cluster.
output_examples.output: Scheduler-aware job templates with placeholders filled for cores, memory, lineage, array ranges, logs, and job dependencies.

output_examples.input: User asks how to report the analysis in a manuscript.
output_examples.output: A concise methods paragraph describing genome sources, BUSCO lineage choice, ortholog filtering, alignments, trimming, model selection, and tree inference.

best_practices: Confirm the BUSCO lineage, taxonomic breadth, and compute environment before generating scripts.
Run generated scripts inside a dedicated project directory with backups of input genome files.
Review all external commands and scheduler settings before submitting jobs or downloading data.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated shell scripts on untrusted filenames without reviewing quoting and command construction.
Do not mix unrelated genome sets without documenting accession sources and sample names.
Do not publish results without checking completeness, ortholog counts, alignment quality, and model fit.

faq.question: Does this skill run BUSCO analyses by itself?
faq.answer: No. It generates guidance and scripts that users can review and run in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it work with NCBI accessions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes helper scripts and guidance for querying and downloading assemblies through NCBI tools.

faq.question: Which schedulers are covered?
faq.answer: The bundled templates cover local execution, SLURM, and PBS or Torque style job scripts.

faq.question: What tools must be installed?
faq.answer: Typical workflows need compleasm or BUSCO, MAFFT, trimming software, FASconCAT-G, IQ-TREE, and ASTRAL.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish as submitted?
faq.answer: Not yet. The audit found eval-based shell wrappers and bundled third-party executable code that need review.

faq.question: Can it help write methods text?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill can draft methods text based on the selected workflow and tool choices.
